item.content:encoded: Thank you everyone for your support and readership, it is much appreciated. This article remains a close and personal piece to me and it's a great feeling to have it well received.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so what you're saying is, i do not recognize the johnny in the water? ;)

i also came here to ask about possible connections to class of 76; i was surprised when it didn't have the tag at the end. but this was still an excellent piece as something that stands on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was sweet. The 4 etchings already made me feel that subtle sense of nostalgia and sadness for some reason, and the interruption message seals it for me. Just made me have a small grin at the end. +1.

+ some very nitpicky stuff
- hide

Okay so, I can be quite dumb when it comes to some stuff, so I do apologize if I sounded clueless in any way.

manufactury

I was wondering if this was an actual word?

And I was a little iffy on how one would accidentally walk into a restricted part of the park? I was wondering if the park authorities would've done something to tell people that that part's not for public use? Idk, it's something very insignificant and compared to the rest of the article, the aforementioned bits didn't detract me from the narrative at all, so kudos to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
4 polaroid photos containing images of 2 unidentified males on a dock. These photos are severely damaged by water.

Alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is not coincidental!

This article originally started as a full-on deep-in-the-weeds '76 piece. As I wrote and iterated I felt that the feel and emotion I wanted to go for were just out of reach and only getting further away. So I stepped back and edited down. This is open in a way that allows it to be connected to '76 without overtly being a '76 piece and the expectations that come with it. For me, the hint that it is allows my mind to wander about what could have happened to Johnny to maintain that hint of horror that underpins the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Summer of '76

Any intentional connection to the Class of the same name or Remembrance? This article occupies a lot of the same emotion-space in terms of nostalgia/tragedy/lost friends, but it could just be coincidental.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Interesting perspective on the author page link, but I really appreciate you saying it. Maybe I can migrate to the "i" box rating module.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I can understand that analysis, it was my intention that the specifics be abstract enough that the reader substitutes their own experience. This was definitely not an intentional lean into the trope, so, just wanted to clear that up and thank you for bringing this to my attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike that the emotional crux of this piece hinges on the Bury Your Gays trope.
Having the ManyMeats authorpage link at the end of the article broke the immersion for me a little bit, but otherwise this was a spectacularly bittersweet anomaly. Loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who's lost a friend I would only see during the summer months, this hits my hard. Well done Meats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done Manymeats. I have a soft spot for short, sweet safe-class anomalies, especially ones that just feel like important human experiences leaving a lasting, unexplained impression on the world. This hits just right.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredibly concise and effective. It's emotive and does everything it needs to in exactly the amount of space needed. Absolutely great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the most compelling short-form stories I've read in a long time.

Summer of '80
Billy Walters
We remember John

This part hit exceptionally hard and in a way that was wholly unexpected. Well fucking done.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Been on my sandbox for a long time, a piece I have struggled to wrap my hands and mind around. Ultimately, an homage to memories of times spent with friends we can no longer talk to in places we can no longer go.

Don't it always seem to go, that you don't know what you got til it's gone.

Image Attribution:
"Vintage Zenith Art Deco Table Radio" by 'Joe Haupt from USA' is licensed under CC BY 2.0
https://ccsearch.creativecommons.org/photos/bd68320b-4a7a-4a0c-8404-4541267838ac

EDIT: 7/1/2020 I have updated the containment procedures to be less "Series 1"-y.
